speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Let me start by checking your blood pressure — it's 154 over 92 mmHg today.

speaker: patient
text: Thanks, doctor. I’ve had a pretty persistent headache since yesterday morning, especially around my temples.

speaker: doctor
text: I see. Any dizziness, nausea, or visual changes with the headache? And have you noticed any fatigue or cold intolerance lately?

speaker: patient
text: Yes — I’ve been exhausted all week, and my hands feel cold even indoors. My skin’s also been dry, and I’ve gained about five pounds without changing my diet.

speaker: doctor
text: Those are classic signs we’ll want to explore further. Let me listen to your heart and check your pulse.

speaker: doctor
text: I’m prescribing Lisinopril 20 mg once daily to help manage your elevated blood pressure. Please take it in the morning with water, and avoid potassium supplements unless we discuss them.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is 84 beats per minute — steady and regular.

speaker: patient
text: That’s reassuring. I did notice my pulse felt faster last night — like my heart was racing when I lay down.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your symptoms and lab trends, we’re diagnosing hypothyroidism. We’ll start levothyroxine soon, but first let’s review your labs.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin is 132 g/L — within normal range for now.

speaker: doctor
text: Wait — just rechecked: your heart rate is now 101 bpm. That’s a noticeable increase from the 84 we saw earlier. Are you feeling anxious or short of breath right now?

speaker: patient
text: A little jittery, yes — and my palms are sweaty. Could that be related to the thyroid issue?

speaker: doctor
text: Possibly — untreated hypothyroidism can sometimes cause paradoxical tachycardia, especially with stress or deconditioning. Let’s hold off on starting levothyroxine until we repeat the TSH and free T4.

speaker: patient
text: Okay. Also, I’ve been feeling unusually pale and tired lately — more than usual for me.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s concerning — your hemoglobin has dropped to 105 g/L since the last draw. We’ll need iron studies and possibly a reticulocyte count to assess for anemia.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been taking over-the-counter iron tablets for the past two weeks because I read low iron can cause fatigue.

speaker: doctor
text: That may explain part of the drop — unmonitored iron supplementation can interfere with thyroid hormone absorption and even suppress erythropoiesis in some cases. Let’s pause it and reassess.

speaker: patient
text: Got it. Should I keep monitoring my pulse at home?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — please track it twice daily and note if you feel palpitations. Also, avoid NSAIDs while on Lisinopril, and report any swelling or cough immediately.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, I checked my pulse again just before coming in — it was back to 84 bpm, same as that reading you took at the start.
